Reported a 25-minute seizure last Friday involving eye deviation, unresponsiveness, and posturing; Diastat was used and perceived as effective. Trileptal increased to 3 mL b.i.d. yesterday. Since shunt revision on 12/18/2007, patient has been sleepier than normal. Became more lethargic six days ago (Monday) with episodes of vomiting and low-grade fevers. Vomiting stopped since hospitalization. Patient is currently lethargic and will not wake up spontaneously, but is appropriate and interacts when awakened. Oral intake is diminished, though she eats well when awake. Less feisty than usual. Seizure-free since admission.

O: Pertinent labs show CRP less than 0.3, CMP normal, and CBC within normal limits. CSF cultures are negative. Dr. Limon's note indicates CSF white blood cell count of 2, 1 RBC, glucose of 55, and protein of 64. No imaging studies are available in the computer; imaging may have been performed at Kaweah Delta Hospital and reviewed by Dr.

A: Patient with history of symptomatic partial epilepsy, static encephalopathy, cerebral palsy, shunted hydrocephalus, and factor V Leiden deficiency presents with recent 25-minute seizure and increased lethargy following shunt revision. Current status includes lethargy, diminished oral intake, and recent vomiting/fevers, now resolved. Labs are largely unremarkable with negative CSF cultures and normal inflammatory markers.

P: Evaluate seizure medication and lethargy. Continue to monitor seizure activity and medication response (Trileptal increased to 3 mL b.i.d.). Assess cause of lethargy and recent vomiting/fevers given history of shunt revision. Review imaging from Kaweah Delta Hospital if available.

P: Admit patient for management of worsening shortness of breath and hemoptysis. Continue current medication regimen including Coumadin, Prevacid, Diovan, Flomax, Coreg, Aldactone, Lasix, and Zocor.

S: 16-month-old male transferred for higher level of care with a 4-day history of penile swelling. The patient underwent circumcision 1 week ago, which was his third circumcision (previous two surgeries were for adhesion removal). He developed fevers (102-103°F) 3 days post-surgery, accompanied by edema and erythema around the circumcision site that spread to the pubic area. He was treated with Tylenol with Codeine and topical antibiotics prior to transfer. Review of systems is positive for constipation (no bowel movements for 2 days), dysuria for 2 days, and foul-smelling penile discharge for 2 days. Negative for runny nose, eye discharge, cyanosis, edema, cough, wheezing, lethargy, seizures, or musculoskeletal pain.

O: Physical exam reveals erythema and edema in the pubic area for the last 3 days. Transfer labs show WBC 15.7, H&H 12.0 and 36. One blood culture was drawn. The patient received a second dose of clindamycin in the emergency room and is status post Rocephin and Cleocin.

A: Pubic cellulitis.

P: Continue current antibiotic regimen (Rocephin and Cleocin). Follow blood culture results.
label: macmini-m4-32gb/mlx-community/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit/soap_en

S: 60-year-old female reports off-and-on almost daily diarrhea, nausea, and inability to eat for the past 10 to 12 months. She presented to the emergency room with worsening abdominal pain and swelling of the right lower leg. She also experienced persistent nausea, headache, and neck pain throughout the hospitalization.

O: Physical examination revealed a diffusely tender abdomen, clear lungs, blood pressure of 129/69, and trace bilateral lower edema. Recent EGD showed retained bile and possible gastritis; colonoscopy revealed diverticulosis. Laboratory studies showed white count 6.7, hemoglobin 13, hematocrit 39.3, and potassium 3.2. A barium enema showed retained stool in the cecum.

A: Nausea and abdominal pain of uncertain etiology. Secondary diagnosis includes migraine headache.

P: Follow up with the physician in the office in 5 to 7 days to resume all pre-admission medications. Diet and activity as tolerated.

A: 26yo G2P1-0-0-1 at 28-1/7 weeks with LLQ pain, constipation (no BM x 2 weeks), nausea/vomiting, and urinary retention.

P: Continue current care at Lyndhurst. Address pain, nausea, vomiting, and constipation. Monitor fetal status.

output_text:
S: Mr. ABC, a 2-year-old boy, presents with complaints of belly and back pain, though he remains active and is eating and drinking well. Parents report a foul odor from the right nose and suspect a retained foreign body, noting a history of a cotton piece removed weeks ago followed by a course of antibiotics. They restarted the remaining antibiotics due to the return of the odor. There is no history of fever, chills, changes in urine or bowel habits, cough, congestion, or pain with urination or bowel movements.

O: Vital signs are stable and the patient is afebrile. General examination reveals a well-nourished, well-developed, pleasant, and cooperative 2-year-old male in no acute distress, appearing healthy and nontoxic. HEENT exam shows normal tympanic membranes and canals. The left naris is normal, while the right naris exhibits a foul odor and questionable purulent drainage.

A: 2-year-old male with history of recent nasal foreign body removal presenting with recurrent foul odor and questionable purulent drainage from the right naris, raising concern for retained foreign body or recurrent infection. Abdominal and back pain reported but patient is acting normally without other acute complaints.

P: Continue monitoring. Consider further evaluation for retained foreign body given the history and current symptoms. Address abdominal and back pain as reported by parents, noting the patient is otherwise acting normally.
label: macmini-m4-32gb/mlx-community/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit/soap_en
